responseSet.title: An Appreciative Interrogation: Commentary on Paper by Mauricio Cortina
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Appreciative Interrogation

Commentary on Paper by Mauricio Cortina

Charles Spezzano PHD

Charles Spezzano

2252 Fillmore Street
San Francisco, CA 94115
E-mail: cjspezzano@aol.com

Charles Spezzano   Personal and Supervising Analyst at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California and a Member of the Los Angeles Institute and Society for Psychoanalytic Studies.

WHAT I WANT TO BRIEFLY DISCUSS FROM MAURICIO CORTINA'S valuable and interesting essay is the notion that objectivity can be usefully defined “as the ability to decenter from our experience and to see it from another person's perspective, not as an ahistorical, Kantian principle.” From the earliest formulation of transference distortion to the most contemporary discussion of jointly created enactments, one thing that continues to matter in psychoanalysis is the tenuous human ability to grasp what is going on in the mind of another person. This is an important ingredient of what we call reality testing, even though that aspect of the definition of reality testing is not always made explicit. In other words, a reality (external to our own minds) that matters to us clinically is, very often, the reality of other minds. Can patients (and can we) recognize that other minds exist (as, at least partially, independent subjects with agency), and can we grasp what they are doing, especially how they represent and understand our personal psychology?

If and when we do grasp another mind, we would be grasping a reality external to us, and so correctly representing in words the unconscious fantasy and emotional state of another person becomes a special instance of the usual meaning of reality testing—that is, the ability to differentiate internal from external reality. It is a special instance in the sense that the external reality we are trying to differentiate from our internal reality is the internal reality of another mind—a reality that is external to us but that, unlike what we usually mean by external reality (e.g., the Earth revolves around the sun), is not external to every mind but only to all but one mind.

617

Editorial Announcement

With this issue Neil Altman will join Stephen Mitchell and Jody Messler Davies as Coeditor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues. Beginning with Volume 10, Davies and Altman will continue on as Editors and Stephen Mitchell will become an Associate and Founding Editor. Anthony Bass will assume the role of Executive Editor of the journal. Owing to overwhelming professional responsibilities, Lewis Aron will not continue in his current position as Coeditor but will remain actively involved with Psychoanalytic Dialogues as an Associate Editor.

At this time we also welcome three new Associate Editors to our staff. They are Margaret Black, Carolyn Clement, and Jay Frankel. We also welcome Stephen Seligman as a new Contributing Editor.

Together, we all plan to continue the tradition of scholarly, innovative, and intellectually challenging presentations for which Psychoanalytic Dialogues has become known.

Alicia Lieberman  

We are Grateful to James Grotstein, Anne Alvarez, and Virginia Demos. Each theorist in his or her own way offered astute, illuminating, and challenging commentaries that have both encouraged and furthered our thinking on the topic of projective identification and its vicissitudes. We were reminded in reading each of the responses of the way in which Freud made little distinction in reference to such concepts as introjection, identification, and internalization. It has been the work of four generations of psychoanalytic theorists to nuance and clarify such concepts in order to have a shared language that is helpful.

We appreciate the way the three respondents made use of our case and discussion in order to further the clarification of these and other psychoanalytic concepts with regard to projective identification. We also take this opportunity to say that, having worked closely with Dr. Seligman for many years, we trust and admire his insistence on theoretical integrity as exemplified by the paper he has written for this volume.

We find all of the comments on the case itself to be on point, and the respondents' observations and interpretations have enriched our understanding of the dyad. We limit our reply to some themes at the level of theory. Specifically, we respond briefly to questions raised regarding relations between events and meaning, trauma, sadomasochism, different types of projective identification, and relations between mtrasubjective and intersubjective experience. Clearly, these themes are not discreet, but overlapping and interrelated.

Event and Meaning

Grotstein and Alvarez remind us of the importance of fantasy, which is unfortunately often underestimated in trauma research.

Annie Sweetnam PHD

Annie Sweetnam

Annie Sweetnam  

I Am Honored To Receive Such A Stimulating And Erudite Response from Dr. Dimen. Her constant nudging toward the realm of the missing, the assumed, or the biased in my work provides me with the opportunity to clarify some of my thinking and some of the differences about psychoanalytic theory and practice that extend beyond differences between Dimen and me.

At first, it appears that Dimen's primary criticism of my article is that it undertheorizes, specifically about the ontological and epistemological status of the body. But, having paused to give thought to this criticism, I then hear Dimen tell me that in fact not only do I have a theory but she knows what theory I have even though I didn't state it. To boot, my assumed theory is a theory that overtheorizes in making absolutist and universal claims. For Dimen, it seems that if one is not an out constructionist then one must be a closet essentialist. She seems to think that she has outed me.

Dimen wishes that I would be clear about my theory and also seems to believe that there are few theoretical possibilities. It is as though she is caught in the dichotomizing she so rails against, as she intimates that one is either an essentialist or a constructionist. Although she cedes that there may be an “in-between” possibility and cites Stein (1992) as an example of someone who has posited such a theoretical position (in-between determinism and constructionism), there is no room or tolerance for a more ambiguous, purposefully fluid theoretical reading of my paper. It is as though she tries to force me to take a stand, to put me into a camp.

Having said that, I shall now take the opportunity to try to spell out more clearly my overall theoretical views as they pertain to my paper and to Dimen's questions and critique—first, concerning my overall approach to clinical material; second, concerning the theoretical status of the body; and, third, concerning the theories of the psychological positions and the erotic third.

[[RunningHead]]

Why Constructivism Will Not Go Away

Commentary on Paper by Mauricio Cortina

Carlo Strenger PHD

Carlo Strenger

Tel Aviv 69123
Israel
E-mail: strenger@freud.tav.ac.il

Carlo Strenger   Faculty Member in the Department of Psychology and the Interdisciplinary BA program in the Humanities, Tel Aviv University, and maintains a private practice.

MAURICIO CORTINA'S INTERESTING AND INSTRUCTIVE PAPER COURA-geously takes a line of thought that is opposed to the current spirit of hermeneutic constructivism. He questions whether we are right in having settled for a conception of psychoanalysis as a playful, loosely organized body of metaphors, narratives, and forms of interaction. Ultimately, Cortina's paper raises the question again whether psychoanalysis is a scientific undertaking that should be grounded on good empirical evidence about causal etiologies, or whether it is a humanistic undertaking primarily concerned with the generation of meaning.

Originally, psychoanalysis was a theory about the causal etiology of psychopathological conditions. I think that the current multiculturalist, constructivist atmosphere of psychoanalysis is a way of coming to terms with the fact that we simply do not have the grand theory we once thought we had. Cortina attempts to reinstate some of the theoretical ambitions of psychoanalysis that have been given up, and he believes that he has identified a candidate for the grand theory in question—attachment theory.

The current pluralistic atmosphere turns psychoanalysis into a playful universe of cultural forms of life. This attitude has turned a necessity into a virtue: The lack of scientific grounding of psychoanalytic thought and practice could leave us with a constant sense that we don't know enough. Instead, it seems to me that the current climate has taken another option. Psychoanalysis is seen as a loosely connected culture of forming the self. It is an essentially humanistic undertaking centered on a ritualized interaction between two people.

609

The Holding Metaphor Embodies a Quality of the Interior While implicitly assuming, indeed relying on, the presence of an exterior. Holding presumes, simultaneously the existence of a background object that holds and the existence of an object that is held. It alludes to form within formlessness, which is suggestive of unfilled, undefined space surrounding an object insufficiently anchored, perhaps floating. Holding creates a buffer between the patient and the “otherness” of the analyst, establishing the conditions for intense affective contact of a particular kind.

The boundaries between interior and exterior and between patient and analyst are simultaneously reinforced and obfuscated during moments of holding, as the analyst attempts, sometimes silently, to enter her patient's experience at a deep level. The analyst's personal presence is felt both more clearly and less distinctly than at other times, for the holding function establishes a very particular kind of object experience. That experience facilitates the emergence or elaboration of affective structure (s) while creating an expanded sense of potentially “fillable” interior space.

It is interesting that the distinction I made between interior and intersubjective dimensions of analytic space finds a parallel in the rich commentaries by Drs. Stein and Ringstrom. Stein's commentary pulled me toward the interior and the metaphoric, allowing me to further elaborate on my own ideas, and Ringstrom's contribution reoriented me toward the intersubjective by raising stimulating conceptual and theoretical issues. My reply, then, shall move from the evocative toward the dialogic in both content and form as I consider first Stein's commentary and then Ringstrom's.

Ruth Stein

Stein's multifaceted and sensitive discussion has enriched and complicated my work by offering other metaphors (e.g., chorion)

Velleda Ceccoli  

I Would Like To Thank Michelle Price For Her Enthusiastic Discussion of my paper, which captures the spirit in which I wrote it. My essay is an attempt to talk about the embodied gender of the analyst and the effect it has on our work—not just on our patients' perceptions and transferences but on the way we listen, speak, and translate experience. It is in fact based on the notion that, as a woman, I hear and process material differently than if I were a man. Elaborating this idea immediately places me in the dilemma of how to write about sexual difference (in the consulting room) as a woman—how to speak from a subjective position while being aware of its construction and shifting meanings—and still address the issue of difference as a meaningful variable in analytic work. Price works within this dilemma and to my mind raises three separate issues as potential points of discussion. I will attempt to address them sequentially.

First, as a postmodern scholar, Price questions the role of biology and anatomy in the conceptualization of difference, focusing on my use of women's capacity for maternity and its meaning as an essential distinction between the sexes. Although at first glance it may appear that I elevate the position of maternity for women, this is not so. I use it as a basic distinction between men and women not only because of the physical reality of it but also because of its many psychic significations. I am trying to elaborate the possible meanings of this position for women analysts in a treatment situation. I do not believe that our capacity for maternity endows us with the ability to mother, to be maternal, or encapsulates the essence of woman. It does, however, influence our experience and that of others on the basis of its meaning. It is an anatomical distinction with a host of meanings and therefore a powerful psychological signifier for both analyst and patient.

713

Recognition of Selfhood as Struggle

Leston Havens MD

Leston Havens

151 Brattle Street
Cambridge, MA 02138

Leston Havens   Professor of Psychiatry at the Harvard Medical School and Cambridge Hospital. He is the author of Approaches to the Mind, Making Contact, A Safe Place, Coming to Life, and Participant Observation.

The experience of selfhood is that of struggle, between conflicting demands, inevitable dilemmas, and shifting allegiances toward deciding what is for us. The complex nature of this struggle often makes the boundaries between health and sickness ambiguous. Therapeutic work is significantly dependent on the capacity to engage with such struggles. This is illustrated by two striking examples of successful work, as well as my own two analyses, and other clinical material.

Reply to Commentaries

Jeanne Bernstein PHD

Jeanne Bernstein

Jeanne Bernstein  

It Is A Privilege To Have A Chance To Respond To Such Different readings of my paper. I consider it a privilege, because no journal except Psychoanalytic Dialogues provides the writer with an opportunity to receive the actual responses to one's work and to have one's very familiar work suddenly come into contact with unfamiliar responses. In this dialogical structure, which Psychoanalytic Dialogues established from its start, the journal emulates the risky and always fascinating task of the analyst when he or she puts forward what is to him or her a rather known conceptualization and receives in return an utterly unfamiliar and surprising response. These moments constitute for me one of the most exciting chances for true learning and remind me of Freud's (1919) idea of the uncanny as well as of Steven Cooper's (in press) description of the analyst as being “hell-bent” in his perilous efforts to intrude upon the patient's solitude.

Let me turn first to Jacobs's more familiar paper. Even though Jacobs thought I had misread his paper, I am very appreciative of his thoughtful response, as in his reply he elaborates in much greater detail his view of intersubjectivity and his understanding of the “highly complex phenomenon” of countertransference. Although I regret that Jacobs felt misunderstood by my reading of his paper and thinks that I have “an established point of view concerning the role of intersubjectivity in analysis and the proper use of the analyst's subjective experiences,” I value the common ground Jacobs found between our respective papers—in particular, his description of the separateness of minds as well as his critique of countertransference as a purely transcriptive instrument. Nonetheless, I would like to take exception with his view that I have an “established” view of intersubjectivity and that I seem to assume in his mind a morally regulating position as to what is “the proper use” of the analyst's subjective experiences.

Letters in Response to Jody Messler Davies's “Between the Disclosure and Foreclosure of Erotic Transference—Countertransference

Can Psychoanalysis Find a Place for Adult Sexuality”

Judith Brisman PHD

Judith Brisman

24 East 12th Street #505
New York, NY 10003

Judith Brisman  

It was a joy to be tangled in the thoughts that Davies's (PD 8/6) wonderfully challenging article inspired. Some questions remain. In

866

particular, I am confused by an implicit intermix of theory. My understanding of Davies1 is that she embraces an appreciation of the postmodern conceptualization of multiplicity in human development and relational expression.

In this most recent article, however, a more linear developmental model is discussed with regard to sexual development. Wouldn't an understanding of “multiple selves” imply that sexual experience and expression could progress quite “normally” under certain conditions but could be pathologically narrowed under other conditions? In the treatment setting, then, one could witness a host of alive sexual experiences within the therapeutic dyad, changing momentarily in a (to use Davies's metaphor) kaleidoscopic manner, and not necessarily in a linear fashion.

How does one integrate an appreciation of multiplicity with a more traditionally linear model of sexual development?

Why Is Attachment in the Air?

Susie Orbach

Susie Orbach

2 Lancaster Drive
London NW3 4HA

Susie Orbach   Cofounder of the Women's Therapy Centre in London and the Women's Therapy Centre Institute in New York. Her latest book is The Impossiblity of Sex. She is a visiting professor at L.S.E.

In the last decade Bowlby's ideas on attachment have been taken up with tremendous flourish within psychoanalysis in general and by feminists, journalists, and politicians. This paper explores this new turn and suggests some historic reasons for the convergence between feminist ideas, politics, and attachment theory. While welcoming the wider dissemination of an attachment paradigm, it cautions against the too easy uptake of ideas that may be less than congruent with the fundamental challenge that attachment theory poses for conventional psychoanalytic models of the mind.

Mauricio Cortina   Faculty Member at the Washington School of Psychiatry and the Institute of Contemporary Psychotherapy, Associate Professor in the Department of Psychiatry, George Washington University, and a member of Seminario de Socio-Psicoanalisis A. C., Mexico City.

I Am Very Thankful To Silverman, Strenger, And Spezzano For Their valuable comments and probing questions. I will start with a summary of the issues they have raised:

Doris Silverman and I share a vision of psychoanalysis in which hermeneutic understanding and empirical research are seen as complementary approaches to the creation of knowledge. The issues Silverman raises are elaborations and correations of that vision.

Carlo Strenger and Charles Spezzano raise several overlapping questions regarding to the place of attachment theory in contemporary psychoanalysis. What makes attachment theory specifically relevant for psychoanalysis? Will attachment become the new paradigm in psychoanalysis? Strenger has his doubts (so do I, but for different reasons, as I discuss later). Strenger and Spezzano ask whether attachment research has made some Kleinian or Freudian developmental theories obsolete. Spezzano asks a related question: Is it necessary to integrate knowledge from other disciplines (e.g., evolutionary theory, developmental theory, the neurosciences, anthropology) before we can have confidence in the psychoanalytic exploration of unconsious fantasy and conflict?

Strenger also questions whether genetic or temperamental factors may account for some of the findings of attachment research.

responseSet.authorMast: Ramon Greenberg and  Chester A. Pearlman
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Interpretation of Dreams

A Classic Revisited

Ramon Greenberg MD and Chester Pearlman MD

Chester Pearlman

11 Waverly Street
Brookline, MA 02146

Chester Pearlman   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Boston University School of Medicine VA Medical Center, Boston, MA.

In this centenary of Freud's The Interpretation of Dreams, it is important to revisit this classic, to discuss why it is a classic, to consider what has been learned since its publication, and to discuss what changes in our understanding of dreams and dreaming are called for. To this end, we briefly discuss some of the main themes of the book. Then we review both changes in psychoanalytic thinking and theory and the results of many studies made possible by the discovery of the electroencephalographic changes that occur during sleep and their relevance for understanding dreams and their function. We suspect that Freud would have been delighted to know about this explosion of information about the physiology of dreaming. With this in mind, we consider the need for modification of some of Freud's theories while noting that his basic contribution, that dreams are meaningful and understandable, has been amply confirmed. We then discuss these observations in relation to how we approach working with dreams.

Milton Kramer MD

Milton Kramer

101 West 79th Street
New York, NY 10024

Milton Kramer   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry at Wright State University in Dayton, Ohio; Volunteer Professor of Psychiatry at the University of Cincinnati; and a Staff Member of the Sleep Disorder Center at Bethesda Hospital in Cincinnati.

The Classic Status and Worldwide Acclaim of Freud's The Inter-pretation of Dreams (TIOD; 1900) is acknowledged by Dr. Sand in her work, “The Interpretation of Dreams: Freud and the Western Dream Tradition,” and by Drs. Greenberg and Pearlman in their article, “The Interpretation of Dreams: A Classic Revisited.” The question arises as to why TIOD is considered a classic. For Greenberg and Pearlman, “The classic status of Freud's book … is based on its new approach to a universal experience” (i.e., dreaming). Sand is of the opinion that “The great acclaim accorded TIOD is evidenced in the erroneous belief … that the very idea of dream meaning had been unsuspected before Freud arrived on the scene.”

What does it mean to say that a work is classic? If we turn to a dictionary (Costello, 1982) for an answer, we find that classic is defined as “of the highest rank or class, an outstanding representative of its kind; model, well known and typical. Having lasting significance or recognized worth, of lasting historical or literary significance.” To be a classic, the “book, picture, music or theory must be of the best, representative of its type, well known and lasting.”

Freud (1900) was of the opinion that TIOD was a work of inestimable importance. He wrote to Fliess on June 12, 1900, after a visit to Belle Vue, the house where he had had the dream called “Irma's injection,” the dream specimen of psychoanalysis: “Do you suppose … that some day a marble tablet will be placed on the house, inscribed with the words? ‘In This House on July 24, 1895 the Secret of Dreams was Revealed to Dr. Sigm. Freud.' At the moment there seems to be little prospect of it” (p. 121).

767

[[RunningHead]]

Bold Analysis and Associative Dialogue

Freedom and Continuity in Analytic Discourse

Uri Hader

Uri Hader

Ramat Aviv 69978
Israel

Uri Hader   Associate Professor and Head of the clinical program at the Department of Psychology, Tel Aviv University. His research interests include the discursive core of analytic psychotherapy, the neuropsychology of language, and coverbal gestures.

Free association allows the emergence of novel formulations about self and, along with them, new insights, but it needs to modulate the risks of chaos and the compromising of mental integrity on the one hand and the arrest of growth on the other. Interpretation acts to contain and hold associative pressures and so helps maintain the patient's mental integrity, but it limits associative freedom by being necessarily inferential. In addition, in current interpretative strategies, analytic progress depends on the patient's ability to associate. This ability may be poor for longer or shorter periods during analysis. To help resolve these difficulties, a strategy of associative dialogue is proposed and clinically illustrated. The proposed strategy draws on the associative abilities of the analyst. This paper elaborates the way in which two basic forms of associative dialogue—bold and focused analysis—may facilitate the negotiation between freedom and continuity in analytic discourse.

Dr. van Zyl: South Africa has been in the media a great deal in this last decade. During this time, it has negotiated a political transition from a draconian apartheid government to a country governed by one of the most enlightened constitutions in the world, a constitution with a clear focus on human rights for all South African citizens. This achievement is all the more remarkable because many in the new government are individuals who personally suffered atrocities and trauma under the old regime, a history which one might not predict would lead to the lack of bitterness, tolerance, and respect for human life which underpins the new constitution and the new government's stated philosophy. However, this constitution was forged by the leaders of the country, and one wonders what is happening on the ground, at the grass roots of South African society. Surely apartheid has left deep psychological wounds? How are these being addressed, and what is the role of South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission [TRC] in this process? Before addressing these questions, however, perhaps you could say a little about yourself and your involvement in the struggle for liberation in South Africa.

Dr. Straker: I am a white South African by birth and have lived my whole life in Johannesburg except for short sojourns in the United States and the UK. I trained as a clinical psychologist and qualified in 1972. I grew up in a sequestered, sheltered world well away from township life, as did most middle-class white South Africans during the 1950s and 60s.

I really only became aware of the political situation in South Africa at the age of 30, when I was promoted to a chair in applied psychology at a largely white university. As the youngest woman to have occupied

Dr. Straker thanks Dr. Melanie Suchet for initiating this paper and for valuable comment and personal support and friendship. She also thanks Dr. Sue van Zyl, her friend and colleague, for interviewing her and the following colleagues for productive discussions and comments on the Truth and Reconciliation Commission: Lesley-Anne Barnett, Gill Eagle, Brandon Hamber, Bronwyn Harris, and Fathima Moosa.

Gerhardt And Borton's Patient Begins Her Response To An Inter-pretation with the word “actually.” The authors point out that this word has no meaning in and of itself, but acquires one only in the context of the conversation in which it's used. In this particular conversation, it conveys a disagreement with and mild criticism of the therapist. It conveys this on a visceral level. The word, itself, through its sound and its context of use, and not because it stands for something deeper, communicates disagreement. Because linguistic “discourse markers” such as “actually” can communicate disagreement, Gerhardt and Borton think that they qualify as examples of what I called the “patient-specific responses” that a therapist could use to know if an interpretation is on the right track. They call this a “soft measure of validation”; because these markers are intrinsically context-dependent, it's a kind of validation that is true to the spirit of postmodern epistemology.

Although analysts might learn something about linguistics from Gerhardt and Borton's paper, I don't think it can help us clinically. Translating the visceral effects of words such as “actually” or “well” into a useful cognitive assessment of the accuracy of an interpretation isn't a practical form of clinical listening. In Gerhardt and Borton's case, for instance, it would be trivial or irrelevant because it's immediately obvious that their patient is asserting a point of view different from the therapists'. She plainly states it. She probably does so with a gusto conveyed by her tone, facial expression, body posture, and general energy level. But she also directly tells the therapist what she's thinking and feeling. It seems odd to tease out the subliminal workings of a discourse marker to explain a meaning that is otherwise transparent.

551

Michelle Price   Psychoanalyst in New York City and Birmingham, MI, a Training and Supervising Analyst at the American Institute for Psychoanalysis of the Karen Horney Institute and Center, a Faculty Member of the National Institute for the Psychotherapies, and a Faculty Member and Supervisor at the Mt. My Father Was A Boxer, As Was His Father Before Him. I Was barred from the ring because I was a girl, and I became an analyst. This and other early experiences have influenced my professional interests, particularly regarding the subjects of aggression and desire in women. Velleda Ceccoli's essay and, most significantly, the analytic story within it, are an attempt to create an innovative way to think and speak about desire and aggression and about their coding in gender. In particular, Ceccoli presents a unique blend of European and American psychoanalytic and feminist thinking to discuss these matters. Such a discussion focuses on the interplay, inhabitation, and embodiment of gender in addition to maternal and paternal orders by men and women, analysts and analysands. This is a topic that interests me on multiple levels, for it addresses an issue that has both intrigued and pained me since I saw the look on my mother's face when I asked my father, on my fifth birthday, for a rifle. I was told girls play with dolls, not guns, to which I responded, “Not this girl. I want to be a cowboy too!” These early experiences contributed to my current interests in the subjects of preoedipal and oedipal maternal and phallic identifications, as well as gender fluidity and rigidity.

Ceccoli's essay (re)visits mothers and fathers, who they are, and what they potentially can provide for us and in us. She speaks to the importance of the paternal and the need for a father who can provide an important presence to his children. This elaborates Benjamin's (1988)

Reply to Commentary

Irene Fast PHD

Irene Fast

337 Lake Park Lane
Ann Arbor, MI 48103
E-mail: fast@umich.edu

Irene Fast  

IT IS A GREAT PLEASURE TO HAVE ADRIENNE HARRIS THINK ABOUT AND comment on my own thinking. Many thanks to her. She emphasizes (rightly, I think) the unresolved complexities in our thinking about sex and gender issues, and the importance, therefore, of attending carefully to the views of those taking positions against the mainstream—such as intersex and transgender groups—while also examining with great care evidence purporting to support one or another controversial position, as illustrated in the John/Joan case.

Harris is correct in her view that I have wanted to place developments in core gender identity in a strongly relational context. It is a relational context I first elaborated as event theory, a conception that, to my considerable pleasure, Harris values. In this conception of gender, however, it seems to Harris that I leave event theory behind. I have no intention of doing that.

In fact, the notion of I-schemes and of a dynamic I-self (and sense of identity) builds directly on event theory. The notion that the dynamic aspects of our psychic structures are composed of personally motivated schemes of interaction is fundamental to event theory. These schemes are composed of all the aspects of an interaction: bodily, mental, perceptual, cognitive, emotional, self and other. Initially all these aspects are inchoate in the global scheme—for example, a scheme of nursing, mutual gaze, or, a little later, peekaboo. Development occurs as the multitude of scheme aspects is articulated in processes of integration and differentiation.

In a recent book, Selving: A Relational Theory of Self Organization (1998), I elaborate the the ways we might understand event schemes as constituting our dynamic I-selves, our personal ways of understanding our experience and acting in our worlds. The present paper explores a further aspect of such schemes—the ways they might be implicated in gender developments of early life.

This paper raises the possibility that, from the beginning of life, our I-Schemes (event schemes) are gendered.

The Third, Minding and Affecting

Commentary on Paper by Lewis Aron

Steven Knoblauch PHD

Steven Knoblauch

30 West 13th Street, #3B
New York, NY 10011
email: sk@psychoanalysis.net

Steven Knoblauch   Faculty and Supervising Analyst, Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity; Faculty and Supervising Analyst, Institute for Contemporary Psychotherapy; Faculty, New York Institute for Psychoanalytic Self Psychology.

ASIGNIFICANT PAPER IN ANY FIELD IS ONE THAT CAN BE LOCATED IN A current or currents of thought and that provokes a number of extensions or expansions in the ideas of others. Lewis Aron situates himself among those authors (Altman, 1995; Kaplan, 1995; Cushman, 1995; Greenberg, 1997; Muller, 1996; Spezzano, in press) who have begun to work out the implications of the embeddedness of the psychoanalytic dyad in the Third and wider field, the “psychoanalytic community and the broader culture” (Aron, this issue). His focus on the development of theory of technique picks up on the contributions of Freud (1927), Fenichel (1941), Eissler (1953), Hartmann (1960), Bergmann and Hartman (1976), Wallerstein (1990), Mitchell (1993), and Kantrowitz (1997), with consideration for what revisions in practice and supervision might emerge from including the influence of the Third. Aron's inclusion of the Third. points to pedagogical as well as political considerations that have hitherto not been central to the discourse of psychoanalysis. Let me begin with pedagogy.

If we accept a shift from “standard technique” to pluralism, as Aron has traced, should we sequentially teach the theory of supervisor X, then the theory of supervisor Y, and then supervisor Z's theory? Or, should we teach all three (or more) treatment models and related treatment techniques, in parallel? If so, should these different perspectives be developed in one comparative course or taught in different courses by instructors competent to represent a particular school of thought?

41

Infant—Parent Interactions, Phantasies, and an “Internal Two-Person Psychology”

Reply to Commentaries

Stephen Seligman DMH

Stephen Seligman

3667 Sacramento Street
San Francisco, CA 94118
seligman@ucsf.edu

Stephen Seligman  

I AM GRATEFUL TO ANNE ALVAREZ, VIRGINIA DEMOS, AND JAMES Grotstein for their discussions. Not surprisingly, each is a contribution in itself, with its own distinctive and formidable voice. Generously and yet critically, they remind us of how much stimulating and challenging work can be done in the areas about which they comment, and they suggest multiple directions with important and intricate implications. Rather than trying to take these up one by one, I will comment on several broad issues. I first emphasize the relationships between Kleinian and intersubjective theories and the “oneperson” versus “two-person” problem in building psychoanalytic models, which are somewhat more explicitly and extensively addressed in Alvarez's and Grotstein's papers, and conclude with some comments about trauma, turning in greater detail to Demos's points.

Infant—Parent Interactions, Phantasies, and “Internal Two-Person Psychology”

From different perspectives, each respondent takes up the issues involved in what Alvarez, with the perceptiveness that pervades her paper, has called “an internal two-person psychology,” a concept that captures a key focus of my own paper. On the theoretical level, I tried to show how intersubjective infant research and modern Kleinian thinking can be brought together to yield a strong psychoanalytic model of psychic life oriented in just such internal two-person structures.

Making Genders

Commentary on Paper by Irene Fast

Adrienne Harris PHD

Adrienne Harris

80 University Place, 5th Floor
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: Aharris@psychoanalysis.net

Adrienne Harris   Faculty Member and Supervisor in the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, and an Associate Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues

In Her Essay, Irene Fast Pushes Her Thinking About Gender Identity in a number of new directions. Her paper, with its quite complex structure and agenda, takes up questions of theory, of ethics, and of practice. This mixture allows us to see that questions of gender go to the heart of identity and personhood and that any decision we make regarding our theories, our practices, or our clinical judgment has powerful moral and political implications.

As there is a lot going on in the essay, let me provide a summary. Fast's strategy is to reread and critique those aspects of Stoller's (1976, 1985) work on gender that stress repudiation of feminine identifications as central to establishing masculinity. Stoller has been a central influence on contemporary gender studies, and Fast uses her rereading to comment on several new developments in the field of gender studies. First, she revisits a notorious case of sex reassignment. Second, she builds on her own work to embed the concept of core gender identity through elaborating the relational matrix in which gender is made meaningful for a child. She ties gender meanings to relationships but also sets this process within the context of unambiguous sex assignment. Third, she takes up the question the relationship of gender to the child's body and body sense and finally proposes a reframing of identification processes in line with the post-Mahlerian views developed in infancy studies—namely, that attachment and individuation are mutually interactive, transforming states.

Fast begins by raising an ethical and clinical question of great importance in her rendering of the case of a gender reassignment conducted and reported by Money.

The Immediate is not Unmediated

Commentary on Paper by Annie Sweetnam

Muriel Dimen PHD

Muriel Dimen

3 East 10th Street, # 1B1
New York, NY 10003

Muriel Dimen   Clinical Professor of Psychology, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; Fellow, New York Institute for the Humanities, New York University; Faculty and Supervisor, Derner Institute Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, Adelphi University.

Many Psychoanalytic Thinkers, Among Them Annie Sweetnam, are currently developing ways to integrate the critique of gender with contemporary clinical theory. Various approaches are taken. Jessica Benjamin (1988), for example, works with gender and intersubjectivity; Adrienne Harris (n.d.) works with gender and psycholinguistics. In focusing on the postmodernist wing of this critique, Sweetnam (1996) has followed Thomas Ogden's theory of psychological experience. Key to psychic structure and clinical process, the three psychological positions he proposes function, in Sweetnam's interpretation, as a screen through which gender passes to assume shape, substance, and meaning. Having argued that gender is constructed and fluid, she now adds sexuality to the discursive stew and posits a stopping point, a point of reference: “the gendered body.” The substantial, material body, she quite rightly contends, must make us stop and think, especially when it comes to the ways corporeality is sensed, for psychic reality is after all the meat and potatoes, not to mention the bread and butter, of our daily clinical lives. In the matter of sexuality, she is saying, we need to think about felt experience as contoured by the body and as it lives through gender. To that end, she models a new concept on Ogden's analytic third: the erotic third. In the course of considering this contribution to psychoanalytic gender theory, I will have occasion to glance at the other, wide-ranging clinical and theoretical issues on which this protean topic inevitably touches.

On both theoretical and clinical counts, this interest in recovering the subjectivized body is timely. Theoreticians have come to recognize a glaring aporia on the deconstructionist page.

Reply to Commentaries

Bruce Reis PHD

Bruce Reis

Bruce Reis  

Stolorow, Orange, and Atwood criticize me in their commentary for having suggested a connection between their work and self psychology. I quote from Atwood (1989):

In speaking of psychoanalytic phenomenology I shall be referring to the framework of psychoanalytic ideas elaborated by Atwood and Stolorow (1984) and Stolorow, Brandchaft and Atwood (1987). This framework, emphasizing the perspective of intersubjectivity, was profoundly influenced by Kohut's works (1971, 1977, 1984) and embraces his most central ideas [p. 194n].

More to the point of my piece, I think it's really unfortunate that Stolorow and his colleagues did not directly comment on Merleau-Ponty's ideas in their reply and that he is nowhere mentioned in their works because I think these ideas, which I attempted to explicate in my piece, could ground the post-Cartesian vision of analytic intersubjectivity toward which these authors seem to be striving.

But to respond to Stolorow et al.'s version of my reading of their work would, I think, bring me into a discussion of the multiple and shifting influences on their intellectual development and how those foundations have or have not lead them to realize the goals of their intersubjective conception. As my citation of Stolorow and his colleagues' work was peripheral to my piece on Ogden, I will take up their commentary vis-à-vis the latter's work.

One of the features I most appreciate about Ogden's intersubjective model is its ability to protect the area of privacy for both analyst and analysand. Ogden (1994) has extended and elaborated on Winnicott's paradox that there is no such thing as an infant (apart from maternal provision) by reincluding the other pole of the paradox: “There is obviously an infant and a mother who constitute separate physical and psychological entities.

[[RunningHead]]

Integrating Kleinian Theory and Intersubjective Infant Research

Observing Projective Identification

Stephen Seligman DMH

Stephen Seligman

3667 Sacramento Street
San Francisco, CA 94118
seligmn@itsa.ucsf.edu

Stephen Seligman   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Infant–Parent Program, University of California, San Francisco; Personal and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; and Faculty, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

THIS PAPER MAKES A SERIES OF INTERTWINED POINTS. AT THE theoretical level, it explores the possibilities for integrating Kleinian theory with intersubjectively oriented infant observation research: Infant researchers' emerging conceptualization of the nonverbal, internal, two-person relationship model as a basic element of psychic structure illuminates the Kleinian concept of phantasy. I apply this approach specifically to consider the concept of projective identification.

This effort seems quite timely. After decades of neglect, Kleinian concepts are attracting avid attention in North American psychoanalytic centers. But there is still considerable uncertainty about them, including the impression that they are inconsistent with the recent relational innovations. I hope to show that this impression is exaggerated and that instead, attention to the Kleinian concepts along with the intersubjective point of view allows us to think simultaneously in “one-person” and “two-person” terms. In this way, the intersubjective models can be extended to capture the density and variety of insights about the inner world in the contemporary Kleinian literature.

Thanks to Robin Silverman and Michael Windholz for their contributions during the conceptualization of this paper. Philip Aranow, Peter Goldberg, Charles Spezzano and David Wallin also provided especially helpful readings.

129

[[RunningHead]]

Interior Experience Within Analytic Process

Joyce Slochower PHD

Joyce Slochower

15 West 75th Street
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: SLOCHOWR@aol.com

Joyce Slochower   Professor of Psychology, Hunter College and the Graduate Center, City University of New York; Visiting Professor and Supervisor at the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; Faculty, Object Relations Institute, National Institute for the Psychotherapies, and the National Training Program; and Visiting Faculty, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California.

In this paper, I consider the feeling of interiority as it evolves within the treatment relationship. A capacity to access and sustain one's interiority reflects a sense of personal solidity within which the validity of subjective process and privacy is taken for granted. When this capacity is relatively undeveloped, individuals rely on the “other” (including the analyst) to help them contact, elaborate, or manage their affective experience. Quite paradoxically, the analyst's active investigation of dynamic or intersubjective process may obfuscate rather than clarify this core difficulty. I suggest two alternative approaches to the treatment situation that stand in some tension and yet also complement each other. One emphasizes the “active” investigation of dynamic and dyadic process, wherein the analyst works interpretively and/or around relational issues. The other is organized around the “interior” dimension of the treatment experience, emphasizing the patient's need to develop or manage her affective process in the relative absence of input from the analyst. Two clinical situations are described, the first illustrating the use of silence with a patient whose difficulties involved affect articulation, and the second involving a patient whose need for affect regulation made her highly dependent on the analyst for soothing.

Bakhtin and Winnicott

On Dialogue, Self, and Cure

Beatriz Priel PHD

Beatriz Priel

Beer-Sheva 84105, Israel
Fax: 07-6900046
E-mail: bpriel@bgumail.bgu.ac.il

Beatriz Priel   Associate Professor of Clinical Psychology, Department of Behavioral Sciences, Ben-Gurion University of the Negev, Beer-Sheva, Israel.

This paper develops a Bakhtinian dialogical perspective on the psychoanalytic discourse in general and on the concepts of true and false selves (Winnicott, 1960b) in particular. Bakhtin's assumptions about the origins of dialogicality in children's development are compared to Winnicott's ideas about the origins of true-and false-self processes. This comparison leads to a characterization of the false and true selves as different genres of the narrated self—the epic and the novel—each with its specific configurations of experience and temporality. Moreover, psychoanalysis is conceived as a unique phenomenon that centers on the internal and most of the time simultaneous dialogues that take place in each of the two participants. This perspective underscores the impact of the analyst's subjectivity on the analytic process as well as the multiplicity of the patient's and the analyst's selves. In this context, therapeutic change can be seen most of the time as a transformation of genre and not necessarily as a modification of contents. Free association allows for the transgression of the basic rules of narrativity, thus facilitating a generic shift. A dialogical relation between the openness of free association and narrativity's coherence is suggested.

Life by its very nature is dialogic. To live means to participate in dialogue [Bakhtin, 1963, p. 293].

A word like “self” naturally knows more than we do [Winnicott, 1960a, p. 158]

[[RunningHead]]

The Interpretation of Dreams

Freud and the Western Dream Tradition

Rosemarie Sand

Rosemarie Sand

455 Ridgeway
White Plains, NY 10605
E-mail: sand2@mindspring.com

Rosemarie Sand   Retired psychoanalyst, a Member of the Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research, and a Member of the International Psychoanalytic Association. She is writing a book on hitherto unexamined relationships between some of Freud's key concepts and psychological theories of the old and sophisticated Central European culture to which he belonged.

Freud's characterization and dismissal of the “symbolic dream interpreting” of his time—his idiosyncratic distinction between that metaphoric form of interpretation and what he called the “scientific” mode, and his subsequent asymmetrical focusing on the latter—seriously skewed his presentation of the nature and history of the Western dream tradition. It obscured the facts that an old theory regarded the dream as the unmasker of the hidden psychic life and that a venerable hypothesis that it could bring to light licit and illicit unconscious desires had not been forgotten by Freud's fin de siécle contemporaries. For many years, his injunction against the use of symbolism, except in the case of the sexual, had an inhibiting effect on psychoanalysts' free use of manifest dream interpretation.

The Analyst's Professional Self as a “Third” Influence on the Dyad

When the Analyst Writes About the Treatment

Margaret Crastnopol PHD

Margaret Crastnopol

515 28th Avenue East
Seattle, WA 98112

Margaret Crastnopol   Associate Director of the Northwest Center for Psychoanalysis, Seattle; a Clinical Instructor in the Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences and in the Department of Psychology, University of Washington; a Supervisor of Psychotherapy and a faculty member of the William Alanson White Institute for Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, New York City; and a Contribuing Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

The analyst's identification with his or her profession and its ethos may profoundly influence the patient's treatment in subtle and less subtle ways. A particularly noteworthy instance of this is the impact of the patient's knowing that he or she is the subject of the analyst's writings. I present illustrative material from a patient in intensive psychotherapy who had pronounced reactions, especially evident in her dream imagery, to my having written about her. This material and the issues it highlights are discussed from the vantage point of the analyst's professional self and psychoanalytic discourse, which together constitute a “third” dimension vis-à-vis the therapeutic relationship. I consider what a Lacan-inspired perspective (Muller, 1996) might contribute to a relational understanding of the impact of the analyst's professional aspect.

Bruce Reis   Candidate in the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. He is in private practice in New York City.

The author situates the development of Ogden's clinical theory over the past 20 years within a philosophic scheme. Ogden (1986, 1989), in using Hegel to deconstruct the Cartesian objectivist assumptions of classical psychoanalysis, goes beyond Hegel to create in Subjects of Analysis (1994) a new synthesis that is similar to aspects of Merleau-Ponty's phenomenology of intersubjectivity. This regrounding allows for the introduction of the analyst's prereflective and sensuous experience through reverie in the cocreation of an intersubjective “third.” The author illustrates how Ogden's solution to the problem of alterity transcends the debate over one-person versus two-person psychologies by producing a truly dialectical, postclassical psychoanalytic theory of intersubjectivity that is neither a one- nor a two-person psychology.

Our New Royal Road to the Unconscious?

Jeanne Bernstein PHD

Jeanne Bernstein

1808 B 4th Street
Berkeley, CA 94710
jwbernstein@msn.com

Jeanne Bernstein   Advanced candidate at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California, San Francisco. She is on the adjunct faculty at The Wright Institute, Berkeley.

In this paper I am tracing the history of countertransference and how it has informed the current debate about self-disclosure as a pivotal instrument of analytic work. Now that the analyst's “subjective factor” has been understood as a central influence on the analysand and as a vital source of information about the analysand's intrapsychic life, I argue that certain currents in the relational school of psychoanalysis confuse the analyst's subjectivity with his personality. While becoming more “real” with a patient may enliven a stale analytic dialogue, it ought not be confused with, or take the place of, an analysis of unconscious desires and phantasies. I claim that a two-person psychology can exist only within a tripartite structure in which the analyst does not lose sight of his complex function of being the carrier, observer, and conveyor of the unconscious currents holding both participants in check.

Sex, Psychoanalysis, and the Moment Before

Muriel Dimen PHD

3 East 10 Street, Suite 1B1

Muriel Dimen   Clinical Professor of Psychology, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; Fellow, New York Institute for the Humanities, New York University; Faculty and Supervisor, Derner Institute Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, Adelphi University.

Recent contributions to the psychoanalytic literature suggest that the classical focus on psychosexuality has been lost. This charge is both wrong and right. After briefly surveying the evolution of psychoanalytic thinking on sexuality and reviewing the concept of libido, this essay retrieves the word Lust from the footnotes to which Freud consigned it and remodels it into a new idea. The proposition is put forth that a postclassical theory of sex compatible with contemporary clinical and theoretical practice can emerge once sexuality is rethought in the ambiguous, potential space between Lust and libido.

The only appropriate word in the German language, Lust, is unfortunately ambiguous and is used to denote the experience of a need and of its gratification.

[Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality, Freud, 1905b, p. 135, fn. 2, added 1910]

Sexuality Is Alive And Well And Living In Psychoanalysis. You just have to know where to look for it—conduct a treasure hunt in which the prize is hiding in plain sight. At the same time, the reports of sexuality's death are not exactly wrong either. This apparent contradiction, really a paradox, deserves some unpacking. After reexamining the idea of libido, I will unearth the idea of Lust

This paper was originally delivered at the Colloquium on Sexuality in Psychoanalysis, Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, New York University, March 1998. I thank Ken Corbett, Jack Drescher, Samuel Gerson, Karol Marshall, and Sue Shapiro for many useful suggestions regarding the present version of this paper.

Attachment Theory and the Psychoanalytic Tradition

Reflections on Human Relationality

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

251 West 71st Street, #1B
New York, NY 10023

Stephen Mitchell   Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, is a Training and Supervising Analyst at the William Alanson White Institute and is on the faculty of the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

I take this occasion to reflect on the relation between the flourishing attachment tradition established by Bowlby's work and the psychoanalytic tradition. I want to show how Bowlby's virtual expulsion from mainstream psychoanalysis in the early 1960s makes some sense in terms of the history of psychoanalytic ideas, as he was several steps ahead of his own time. I then consider the ways in which other major psychoanalytic authors, W.R.D. Fairbairn and Hans Loewald, as well as the American psychiatrist Harry Stack Sullivan, struggled with the same problems Bowlby did. There is now a marked receptivity among psychoanalysts to the same ideas that earned Bowlby his place, along with Fairbairn and Sullivan, in the psychoanalytic Gulag. Finally, I consider the way in which an integration of these different angles on the same phenomena can be helpful both conceptually and clinically.

In this essay, I argue that it is in the very process of establishing a personal relationship to psychoanalytic theory and to the profession that one becomes a psychoanalyst. If our identities as psychoanalysts are established through our relationships to the psychoanalytic community, and to the values and ideals established by this society and embodied in its theories and practices, then psychoanalysis itself functions as the Third to analysts' dyadic relationships with their patients. Although contemporary psychoanalysis emphasizes the subjective, the intersubjective, and the unique relational matrix, the view presented here of the psychoanalytic relationship as always existing within the context of the psychoanalytic community and its history provides a context that allows us to place some (relatively objective) constraints on what we mean by psychoanalysis, and these restrictions are imposed not only by the individuals directly involved (subjectively) but by a wider set of forces, including not only contemporary influences, but the voices of history and tradition as well. Idiosyncratic, personal, and subjective factors (the romantic vision of psychoanalysis) must be continually counterbalanced by more general, objective, and impersonal considerations (a classicist vision), and, among the analyst's many professional responsibilities, one is to manage the polar tension between these forces. The analyst's only hope in managing this tension comes from the very fact that he or she can rely on the sustaining background of professional allegiances to theory and community, psychoanalytic values and beliefs. The exploration of a clinical vignette from the perspective of three different supervisors illustrates how the theoretical

Aspects of Core Gender Identity

Irene Fast PHD

Irene Fast

337 Lake Park Lane
Ann Arbor, MI 48103
E-mail: fast@umich.edu

Irene Fast   Professor of Psychology (Emerita) at the University of Michigan.

Stoller proposes two fundamentally contradictory conceptions of core gender identity. This paper urges that we accept the first, a relational conception: Core gender identity develops in the context of unambiguous sex ascription at birth and the virtually infinite number of daily infant—parent interactions permeated by all the conscious and unconscious meanings the child's gender has for its parents.

The implications of this conception are explored in four contexts. The first shows that a recent case of sex reassignment, purporting to demonstrate a neurological base for gender experience, does not do so. The second argues that children's core gender identities as “girls” or “boys” are as diverse as the meanings that their genders have had for their interaction partners (usually the parents). The third emphasizes the priority of infant—parent interactions in core gender identity development and in the gendered meanings that children's genitals have for them. The fourth argues for the rejection of the “disidentification” hypothesis rooted in Stoller's second and unsupported conception of core gender identity and in Mahler's conception of separation-individuation, which might usefully be replaced by Lyons-Ruth's conception of attachment-individuation.

The Third as Holding the Dyad

Commentary on Paper by Margaret Crastnopol

John Muller PHD

John Muller

Stockbridge, MA 01262

John Muller   Director of Education, Erikson Institute for Education and Research, Austen Riggs Center, Stockbridge, Massachusetts.

The Issues Raised By Dr. Crastnopol's Paper Provide An Oppor-tunity to reflect on relational theory. As I have tried to express in the book she cites (Muller, 1996), a relationship cannot stand on its own but must be anchored in a wider context of culture and semiotic code that can be called the Third. For the psychoanalytic dyad, the Third functions as the holding environment setting standards, defining tasks, and establishing roles for each participant in the dyad. From this point of view, the Third is other than the members of the dyad and is not reducible to simply another dyadic relation with one or each of the members of the dyad.

I believe this notion is already present in Freud's delineation of the oedipal resolution. The child has a relationship to the mother, the child has a relationship to the father, and the child may have a relationship to God; in none of these is the function of the Third explicitly acknowledged. In the oedipal triangulation, the child's relation to the mother is itself, as a relation, related to the child's relation to the father; further, the child's relation to each of the parents is in turn related to the parents' relation to each other. Even more complex, the child's relation to the parent of either sex is itself related to how each of the parents relates to the standard or ideal of sexual identity, as well as of parental identity, which stands apart from each of them. What I am getting at is that the oedipal resolution or triangularity as such does not involve a set of polar dyadic relations but in fact something quite more substantial if not more abstract—namely, a relation of relations.

As I have been invited to comment on Dr. Reis's Essay, I will take the opportunity to respond briefly to his remarks on my use of Hegel's concepts in order to clarify my position. Reis states that, in my model, “self—consciousness moves … from a condition of solipsism to a relational position,” but he objects to this conception of movement, pointing out that the

recognition of the other as a separate yet related other as an end point in itself is insufficient. Interdependence, established through identification with the other, is not yet intersubjectivity. For Hegel, subjectivity remains equated with the conscious subject in competition with the other … [but] … that competition would already represent a differentiation of subject from object.

Actually, I agree with Reis on both counts—regarding the insufficiency of identification and the fact that competition could be said to presume a prior differentiation. Indeed, I believe these points were the gist of my argument in The Bonds of Love (1988), in which I aimed to differentiate my view of intersubjectivity not only from the Hegelian idea of competition but also from the ego—psychological theory of internalization. Like Ogden, I adopted Hegel's dialectic critically, with a crucial emendation. I stated that the problem with Hegel is that, in his formulation, the self does not want to recognize the other (1988, p. 53) but, as in Freud's notions of how the self relinquishes narcissism, does so because it must. Stephen Mitchell

Stephen Mitchell

Stephen Mitchell   Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, is a Training and Supervising Analyst at the William Alanson White Institute and is on the faculty of the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

As This is My Last Issue as Editor Of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, I'd like to share some final reflections on the 10 years that have passed since this journal was conceived. Journal and journey derive from the same root word, which connotes time and passage, and the past decade has witnessed an extraordinary passage of time in the world of psychoanalytic ideas and clinical practice.

Applied to psychoanalytic theory, “relational” was initially a bridge concept pointing to an implicit congruence in basic presuppositions about mind inherent in what seemed to be the widely divergent traditions of American interpersonal psychoanalysis and British-school object relations theories. Before the opening of that conceptual bridge, the intrapsychic theorizing of the Freudian/Kleinian world and the transactional theorizing of the interpersonal world were truly separate continents. The relational bridge, at first a narrow, unused underground passageway, has become, in the past decade, a conceptual superhighway for exploring the reciprocally generated inter-penetrability of the inner world of subjective presences, ghosts from the past, and internalized object relations, and the external world of intersubjectivity and present, here-and-now interaction. And accompanying this conceptual opening has been a much greater political tolerance for pluralism in psychoanalytic institutes and associations and a much greater interest in comparative psychoanalysis and in the exploration of different perspectives in psychoanalytic journals. Whatever role the journal and journey of Psychoanalytic Dialogues has played in these developments has been extremely gratifying.

Now, as one of the Associate Editors, I look forward to the beginning of a new era of continued vitality in psychoanalytic theory and clinical practice.

responseSet.title: From Holding Receptacle to Interior Space—The Protection and Facilitation of Subjectivity: Commentary on Paper by Joyce Slochower
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Holding Receptacle to Interior Space—The Protection and Facilitation of Subjectivity

Commentary on Paper by Joyce Slochower

Ruth Stein PHD

Ruth Stein

3 Brody Street
Jerusalem, Israel 92502
E-mail: rustein@netvision.net.il

Ruth Stein   Training and Supervising Analyst, Israel Psychoanalytic Society; Member, International Psychoanalytic Association; and Lecturer, Psychotherapy Program, Tel Aviv University Medical School. She holds private practice in Jerusalem.

IN THE TIMAEUS (LINES 49-52), PLATO DESCRIBES “THE RECEPTACLE OF being” in words that he admits are extraordinarily “difficult and obscure” (p. 69). Having presented a powerful image of human generation as a receptacle of becoming, Plato then undermines it, and in a long-winded process of substitutions, replaces the word “receptacle” with “space” (chora).

Joyce Slochower, in her paper, writes about leaving the patient space to release the patient's own sense of interiority, his self-experience of having a living inside. Slochower is known for her important elaboration of the concept of holding (Slochower, 1996) to denote the analyst's functioning as a receptacle for the patient's unbearable split-off affects. Holding in this sense is like softening the contact with the external object, sparing the patient the need to be aware of and relate to the outer, “objective” shape of the object, which is adaptive, resilient background.

In a move strangely reminiscent of Plato's shift, Slochower goes from writing about holding and serving as a receptacle to focusing on space. “Space … is eternal and indestructible … [it] provides a position for everthing that comes to be, and … is apprehended without the senses by a sort of spurious reasoning and so is hard to believe in—we look at it indeed in a kind of dream,” (p. 69) Plato wrote. The wonderment discernible in Plato's words has to do with the primal, vast, nonverbal nature of these notions.

[[RunningHead]]

Toward a Triadic Relational Theory

An Introduction

Lewis Aron PHD ABPP

Lewis Aron

13 Longview Road
Port Washington, NY 11050
E-mail: lewaron@psychoanalysis.net

Lewis Aron   Director of the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis and an Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

The Name Of This JOURNAL, Psychoanalytic Dialogues, Has Been appealing and evocative to many of us because it alludes to a variety of important psychoanalytic conversations. Psychoanalytic Dialogues refers not only to the clinical exchange between analyst and analysand but also to the professional and scholarly discourse among the various schools and traditions of psychoanalytic thought and to the emphasis that this journal places on theoretical exchange and respectful debate—to the actual give-and-take between and among its diverse contributors as well as among its authors and readers.

The following exchange between Margaret Crastnopol and John P. Muller is much in keeping with this spirit of psychoanalytic dialogue in each of the respects just mentioned.

First, Crastnopol provides a lively and accessible account of the analytic conversation between her and her patient, Nancy, so that the reader may acquire a feel for the atmosphere of this analytic engagement.

Second, Crastnopol introduces a dimension of the treatment process that has been largely neglected in the analytic literature—she reports on her clinical experience of writing about Nancy and of getting Nancy's permission to present and publish what she is writing. Even more bravely, Crastnopol reports on the analytic experience in which Nancy reads the written material and reacts to it directly and indirectly with dream material, associations, symptoms, and transference—countertransference enactments.

An Interview with Gillian Straker on the Truth and Reconciliation Commission in South Africa

Sue van Zyl

Sue van Zyl

Sue van Zyl  

Instead of revenge there will be reconciliation.

Instead of forgetfulness there will be knowledge and acknowledgement.

Instead of rejection there will be acceptance by a compassionate state.

Dullah Omar, Minister of Justice, 1996

Introduction by Neil Altman, Adrienne Harris, Glenys Lobban, and Melanie Suchet

CAN PSYCHOANALYSIS SHED LIGHT ON THE DEPTH, THE SCALE, OR THE tenacity of violence in human social life? Will this depthpsychological theory be of use in understanding larger political processes, warfare, oppression, and the range of cruelty and heroic repair that characterizes human response? Actually, we must now ask this question in two directions. How has our evolving psychoanalytic theory been shaped or influenced by the dramatic and terrible events of the past hundred years? It is certainly thought that Freud's views of human capacity were altered by the experiences of World War I. The relationship between war and trauma research is complex and bidirectional. As we try to comprehend the incomprehensible—the Holocaust, Bosnia, Rwanda, the Soviet Union of the Stalinists, China's Cultural Revolution, ongoing terrorism and revenge in the Middle East, intractable vengeance and hatred lived out in tandem with utopian and revolutionary visions and great movements for social justice—we can ask how psychoanalysis can participate in an evolving understanding of political and social forces and events.

245

[[RunningHead]]

“She Thinks of Me as Thinking, and Therefore I Exist as a Thinker”

Commentary on Paper by Lewis Aron

Giuseppe Moccia MD and Gianni Nebbiosi PHD

Gianni Nebbiosi

Via Lorenzo Valla 12
00152 Roma
Italy
Fax: 06-8535-2003

Via Adige 8
00198 Roma
Italy

Gianni Nebbiosi   Scientific Director of ISPS (Istituto per lo Studio Psicoanalitico della Soggettività—Roma) and Member of SIPSÉ (Società Italiana di Psicologia del Sé). He is Corresponding Member of ICP (Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis—Los Angeles).

CONTEMPORARY PSYCHOANALYSIS TENDS TO MOVE TOWARD A MULTI-plicity of theoretical perspectives. This seems to reflect the general crisis of the great narratives of the beginning of the century—narratives through which our vision of the world is viewed.

In the past few decades, the belief in being able to specify scientific, objective, and universal truths has receded. In fact, we are more and more interested in the procedures through which we learn and in the influence that our subjective tendencies, our social and cultural background, have on the nature of what we know.

Differentiation of theories produces widespread fear in many that psychoanalysis is moving toward some sort of “psychotic fragmentation” in which many theories, split from one another, lose the possibility of holding a dialogue and the capability of identifying with psychoanalysis itself, being so different. This is because no theory—by tolerating conflict and contradiction—gives up the idea of representing the whole psychoanalytic movement (Aron, 1996).

Given the situation in psychoanalysis today, it is comforting to meet an author such as Lewis Aron.

53

responseSet.title: Reply to Commentaries
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Commentaries

Lewis Aron PHD

Lewis Aron

Lewis Aron  

IT IS WITH MUCH GRATITUDE TO MY COLLEAGUES THAT I WRITE THIS BRIEF reply to their commentaries on my article. My article is wide ranging in scope and provocative in spirit, and the four commentaries each extended the discussion along divergent lines, opening up what are, to my mind, many of the most significant and compelling questions facing psychoanalysis today. Psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic education at their best are necessarily provocative. The word provoke in fact, has its origins in late 14th–century medicine, where it was used to mean “stimulation” of a patient; derived from the Latin provocare, it means to call forth, to challenge, to appeal, and to excite. As psychoanalysts, we do indeed stimulate and challenge our patients to become increasingly curious and engaged in an intense and extended examination of their lives. We stimulate them by thinking with them about their minds and the analytic process and by affectively engaging them in the analytic relationship. As psychoanalytic educators we, hopefully, excite our students, challenge their thinking, arouse their passion and commitment to the discipline, and bring out their own calling to practice psychoanalysis. I could not be more gratified that my article has been successful in stimulating my discussants to engage in such thoughtful, passionate, well-argued, wide-ranging, and edifying commentary and criticism. In this respect, Psychoanalytic Dialogues has been consistently provocative to our discipline from its inception.

Arnold Cooper challenges me with having radically overstated my case, and yet he himself cites relevant contemporary research that itself demonstrates that, when reviewing transcripts of sessions, even experienced psychoanalysts cannot agree on what constitutes analytic process. It seems to me that such a finding presents us with a radical challenge! Cooper is right, though, to remind us of those background beliefs that we all share, including unconscious motivation, developmental influences, psychic conflict, and clinical curiosity.

Commentary on Paper by Jeanne Wolff Bernstein

Theodore Jacobs MD

Theodore Jacobs

170 East 77th Street, #1G
New York, NY 10021

Theodore Jacobs   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Albert Einstein College of Medicine; Training and Supervising Analyst, The New York Psychoanalytic Institute and the New York University Psychoanalytic Institute; and author of The Use of the Self: Countertransference and Communication in the Analytic Situation.

IN ORDER FOR ME TO RESPOND MEANINGFULLY TO JEANNE WOLFF Bernstein's critique of my article, “The Inner Experiences of the Analyst: Their Contribution to the Analytic Process” (1993), and to her general argument with regard to the role of countertransference in the analytic process, I must explain the circumstances under which that article was written, its purpose as I saw it, and what in it I was—and was not—seeking to convey.

About seven years ago, I was asked by the North American Chair of the Program Committee of the International Psychoanalytical Association to submit to a contest a short piece describing my inner experiences during an analytic hour. The contest was being conducted to choose a presenter for one of the plenary sessions at the association's summer 1993 meeting in Amsterdam.

I agreed to submit a piece as I had been interested in the question of the analyst's subjective experiences in analytic sessions and how they might be used. For that reason, I had kept some fairly detailed notes on several sessions with a difficult and challenging patient and, based on these, I simply set down all that I could remember of what had passed through my mind during one of these sessions. I had no intention of making a statement about the role of countertransference or the analyst's subjectivity in sessions; about how, theoretically, I think about these issues; about the various ways in which I use my subjective experiences in clinical work, about the role of the intersubjective and/or intrapsychic dimensions of analysis, about analytic technique or the therapeutic action of analysis, or about an egopsychological as compared with any other theoretical approach.

301

Reply to Commentary

Margaret Crastnopol PHD

Margaret Crastnopol

515 28th Avenue East
Seattle, WA 98112

Margaret Crastnopol  

DR. MULLER'S ELABORATION ON HIS USAGE OF THE “THIRD” HELPS TO clarify where I may have misread him, which aspects of his formulation I find problematic, and where our underlying beliefs fundamentally diverge. I found edifying Muller's further explanation of how the Third might be said to provide a “structuring context,” one that is “antecedent to” the analytic dyad. In his view, the Third is a “relation of relations” and is manifest in “an observable structure (e.g., the semiotics of turn taking; the deictic use of pronouns; the delineation of roles, tasks, and boundaries) defining a position from which to participate in and observe the dialogue.” This description of the Third is similar to the last facet of my own tripartite version of the Third, to which Muller refers in his response—a facet that he (sometimes) seems to say is the only legitimate usage of the term.

Although I mentioned this in my paper, it may bear reiterating that Muller's own view is neither unified nor altogether clear. Both as articulated in his book and in his commentary here, his Third seems (confusingly) to take on different qualities depending on the particular passage in which it is invoked. In some places, Muller presents the Third as an overarching, preexisting position, formal rather than content-specific, and independent of the particularities of a given analytic pair. Yet elsewhere, Muller embraces conceptualizations like those of Ogden and Brickman, who present the Third as something neither universal nor free of the participants' subjective perspectives. Ogden's “analytic third” describes “the intersubjectively-generated experience of the analytic pair.” Similarly, Brickman invokes the Third to encompass “the analyst's deployment of a working model of a dynamic unconscious.” These conceptualizations are like mine in addressing the highly subjective aspect of the Third, which is uniquely and freshly created within and between the members of each analytic dyad.
