The Pure Gold of Natural Language

Commentary on Michael J. Bader's “Postmodern Epistemology: The Problem of Validation and the Retreat from Therapeutics in Psychoanalysis”*

Julie Gerhardt PHD and Leeann Borton MA

Leeann Borton

1453 Mission Street
San Francisco, CA 94103

Leeann Borton   Doctoral candidate at the California Institute of Integral Studies and a member of the Northern California Society for Psychoanalytic Psychology. She is in private practice as a psychological assistant in San Francisco.

In Response To A Previously Useful Interpretation About Her continued need to “keep us [her and her therapist] confused” about whose desire is being expressed when older men come on to her, which they reportedly continually do, a patient recently countered, “Actually, I can now begin to feel my own desire for these men,” in a tone so newly confident and subtly corrective that the therapist immediately associated to the temerity of certain opera singers who, after cutting their teeth on Puccini, find themselves wanting to perform Wagner! On stage, no less! The therapist became aware of straining ever so slightly in her chair, as if expecting to hear something new, and found herself somewhat excited. At the same time, she felt a bit chastened by the patient for her “hackneyed” (Ogden, 1997a) thinking. In Bader's terms, without being consciously aware, the therapist was in the midst of allowing her original interpretation qua hypothesis to be disconfirmed and corrected by the patient's voice—itself part of a performative display of her own desire—as part of their ongoing effort to better understand the patient's psyche, the much contested “it” that is or is not “there” to be understood.

Michael J. Bader's paper appeared in Psychoanalytic Dialogues, (8/1).

An earlier version of this paper was published as Gerhardt and Borton (1998).

[[RunningHead]]

Dreams Contextualize Emotion—A New Way of Understanding Dreams and Dream Symbolism

Commentary on Papers by Rosemarie Sand and by Ramon Greenberg and Chester A. Pearlman

Ernest Hartmann MD

Ernest Hartmann

Newton-Wellesley Hospital
27 Clark Street
Newton, MA 02459-2425

Ernest Hartmann   Professor of Psychiatry at Tufts University School of Medicine and Director of the Sleep Disorders Center, Newton-Wellesley Hospital, Boston. He is the author of 300 published articles and eight books, most recently Dreams and Nightmares: The New Theory (New York: Plenum Trade Books, 1998).

I Would Like to Begin Not with a Detailed Look at the Papers By Dr. Sand and by Drs. Greenberg and Pearlman, or even with Freud's interpretation of dreams, but rather with some striking images I have repeatedly found in long series of dreams collected from persons who have gone through a traumatic or disturbing event. I am not talking about the clinical condition posttraumatic stress disorder but rather about the more common situation in which someone has just escaped from a fire, has been raped or attacked, or has experienced the unexpected death of a loved one and is gradually coming to terms with the event. What I find in these dreams is what I call the “tidal wave” dream. No matter what sort of trauma the person has experienced, the dreams during the following weeks often contain something like this: “I was walking along the beach, when suddenly a huge tidal wave came up and engulfed me. I was thrown around, terrified, not sure whether I would make it to the surface when I woke up.”

The subject is not always specifically a tidal wave, of course, but I call these dreams tidal wave dreams after one of the most prominent images. Other images that come up frequently include “A huge train was rushing right at me … I couldn't get out of the way. I woke up terrified” and “I was being chased by a gang of thugs or Nazis, and I couldn't get away.

779

Letters in Response to Jody Messler Davies's “Between the Disclosure and Foreclosure of Erotic Transference—Countertransference

Can Psychoanalysis Find a Place for Adult Sexuality”

Ruth Imber PHD

Ruth Imber

390 West End Avenue
New York, NY 10024

Ruth Imber  

I found the article by Davies (PD 8/6), as well as the commentaries by Cooper and by Gabbard and Hoffman, and Davies's reply, all thoughtful and stimulating (as far as I can tell, only on an intellectual level). However, I was surprised by what seems to me a glaring omission in all the proceedings. I do recognize, with Cooper, that “the patient and analyst whose work is being described know each other far more intimately than the reader to whom the analysis is being described” (p. 767). At the same time, I believe there are unconscious feelings and thoughts that ensure that even the most intimate of relationships will always contain elements of the unknown and mysterious for both parties.

It is because of my respect for the abiding power of the unconscious that I was troubled by Davies's apparent assumption that she knew what dyadic fantasy constellation was sexually stimulating her patient David at the point when she disclosed her flirtatiousness to him. We are not told what David's associations had been before his comment, “Forgive me if I'm wrong, but I think you're flirting with me.” Nor are we told just what the analyst had been saying or doing that constituted “flirting.” What we are told is that Davies immediately recognizes the validity of David's observation. We are further told that Davies doesn't seem to have any hesitation in assuming she knows who she is to her patient at this moment. She is the mother.

Jody Messler Davies's paper appeared in Psychoanalytic Dialogues (8/6). Jody Messler Davies, Ph.D. is Co-Editor, Psychoanalytic Dialogues; Supervising Analyst and CoChair of the Relational Track, New York University Postdoctoral Program Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis and Faculty and Supervisor, National Institute for the Psychotherapies.

Response to Ruth R. Imber, Ph.D.

It was one of my intentions in writing this paper to think through a more contemporary conceptualization of the Oedipus complex—one not rooted in the more hegemonically normative distinctions between paternal and maternal identifications, positive and negative oedipus, heterosexual and homosexual object choice. In conceptualizing the move from oedipal to postoedipal sexual functioning as encompassing a movement from omnipotent romantic idealization to the realm of actual, experienced erotic agency in both the subjective and objective positions, I had hoped to offer an alternative vision of oedipal issues that operated independent of gender identifications and sexual object choices.

Commentary on Paper by Jeanne Wolff Bernstein

Lynne Layton PHD

Lynne Layton

1101 Beacon Street, Suite 6 West
Brookline, MA02446
layton@fas.harvard.edu

Lynne Layton   Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychology, Department of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School at the Beth Israel-Deaconess Medical Center; visiting faculty, Committee on Degrees in Women's Studies and Committee on Degrees in Social Studies, Harvard University; and faculty, Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Dr. Bernstein's Paper Raises Some Of The Most Crucial Questions one can ask about the potential pitfalls of the way the relational, intersubjective, and interpersonal schools conceptualize not only countertransference but therapeutic action in general.1 The concerns she so cogently presents and analyzes in depth are concerns I have heard expressed at just about every conference I have been to in recent years; her paper is thus an important contribution that surely will enable those with misgivings about the two-person paradigm to ground their misgivings in the theory Bernstein provides. For me, the two most significant questions Bernstein raises are:

Does the two-person paradigm do away with the unconscious? Suggesting that it does, Bernstein appears to be following in the tradition of Lacan, who harshly criticized ego psychology for abandoning Freud's most radical discovery: the dynamic unconscious.

1Although there are many differences between these schools, in what follows I refer to the target of Bernstein's critique as either the two-person or the relational paradigm. I am grateful to Susan Fairfield (personal communication) for pointing out the confusions that arise from the very different ways that Lacanians and Anglo-Americans use the same words, especially intersubjective, ego, and relational (a helpful guide to Lacanian terminology is Evans, 1996). Although the Lacanians understand their theory to be intersubjective and relational, my sense it that they embed these terms in what the two-person paradigm would call a one-person theory. Although Bernstein agrees with much of the relational paradigm's critique of the classical paradigm, her theorizing, too, endorses a one-person perspective.307

[[RunningHead]]

Performativity, Transsexualism, and Benevolent Psychopathology

Some Psychoanalytic Reflections on Postmodernist Views of Sexuality

Jennifer McCarroll MA

Jennifer McCarroll

3201 Speedway, #1
Austin, TX 78705
E-mail: j_mccarroll@yahoo.com

Jennifer McCarroll   Doctoral Candidate in Counseling Psychology at the University of Texas at Austin.

Postmodern theory and critiques currently exert a considerable influence on psychoanalytic theory, particularly in regard to the revision of a theory of sexuality and gender. This article reviews some of the critiques and alternative views of sexuality proposed by various postmodern theorists such as Butler, Jameson, Baudrillard, and Foucault. After conducting a critical comparison of these critiques and alternatives with contemporary analytic revisionist theory, I contend that, despite the useful aspects of postmodernism's critical cultural perspective, some postmodern positions run directly counter to certain key psychoanalytic tenets about human agency and the existence of an inner psychic world or selfhood. Furthermore, I believe that, despite the laudable politics of many postmodern writers to promote a greater diversity of nonpathologized sexual experience, the atrophied views of agency and selfhood depicted in the postmodern writings reviewed in this essay cannot support an approach that is concerned with clinical treatment or with describing processes of psychological transformation. A case vignette is provided illustrating the main arguments of the paper.

Exploring the Patient's “Interiority”—When to Engage and When to Hold

Commentary on Paper by Joyce Slochower

Philip Ringstrom PHD Psy.D

Philip Ringstrom

5004 Haskell Avenue
Encino, CA 91436
E-mail: ringsite@aol.com

Philip Ringstrom   Training and Supervising Analyst and Faculty Member, Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles, and a Panelist on the Psychoanalysis Broadcast Network (PsyBc.com).

In her 1996 Volume, Holding and Psychoanalysis: A Relational Perspective, Slochower expressed her conviction that the relational psychoanalysts' emphasis on mutuality may be experienced as noxious by patients who find the articulation of certain affects unbearable or for whom intersubjective relating is beyond their capacity. For these patients, therapeutic restraint in the form of “holding” was prescribed. In the current article, Slochower extends this thesis to cover not only these conditions but also clinical contexts in which mutual engagement inhibits the emergence of the patient's experience of “interiority”—that is, to be able to hold onto one's own experience of solidity without requiring external validation or recognition.

Although the relational perspective is much broader than this active-engagement caricature, as Slochower (1996) acknowledged it is often in its departure from previous traditions that a new perspective becomes most prominently known (Aron, personal communication).1 Hence, one frequently hears of the relational perspective as involving doggedly curious exploration (Mitchell, 1988, 1993, 1997), analyst's self-disclosure (Davies, 1994; Aron, 1996), mutual recognition (Benjamin, 1988, 1992, 1995), and acts of spontaneity such as “throwing away the book” (Hoffman, 1994, 1998) and improvisation (Ringstrom, 1997).

1In fact, a careful reading of relational authors such as Bromberg (1998) reveals careful attempts of moving between activity and restraint in a manner that facilitates opening the “analytic space.”825

Bridging Natural Science and Hermeneutics: An Oxymoron or a Conjunction?

Commentary on Paper by Mauricio Cortina

Doris Silverman PHD

Doris Silverman

315 Central Park West
New York, NY 10025
E-mail: DorisKSilverman.compuserve.com

Doris Silverman   Faculty Member in the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis and a Training Analyst, Supervisor, and Teacher at the Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research (IPTAR) a component society of the International Psychoanalytic Association (IPA). She is on the editorial boards of Psychoanalytic Dialogues and Psychoanalytic Psychology.

As One Who Has Attempted Integrations Of The Meanin-Making function of clinical work with the systematic search fof generalities about the mind, I appreciate Mauricio Cortina's thorough, thoughtful, and scholarly approach to the topic. He merits praise for his attempts to synthesize goals and for his careful and reasoned supportive arguments. Cortina's willingness to struggle against the pull of postmodernism in current psychoanalytic thinking is laudatory. The so-called demise of any natural science orientation is only a hot, contagious rumor. Cortina has presented an important antidote for this epidemic.

Cortina is addressing the various philosophical stances in our field. I detail these first because, in our theorizing, we can wittingly or unwittingly shift from one stance to another and I believe such moves implicitly (or explicitly) inform our clinical thinking and actions. Second, I address certain extra-analytic thinking that has emerged from bioneurological (Edelman, 1992) and psychological (Thelen and Smith, 1995) research and that has begun to transform the way we think about development (i.e., science influencing our clinical theory). Third, I address aspects of the attachment system as it interfaces with this new developmental perspective.

597

Editorial Announcement

Stephen Mitchell

Stephen Mitchell

Stephen Mitchell  

With this issue, Lewis Aron and Jody Messler Davies join me as Editors of Psychoanalytic Dialogues. The three of us will collaborate as Editors of volume 9. Beginning with volume 10, Aron and Davies will continue on as Editors and I will become an Associate (and Founding) Editor.

Lewis Aron has been involved with Psychoanalytic Dialogues since its creation in 1990, and Jody Messler Davies since 1994. They represent what is for me the essence of what we have tried to create through PD, a combination of deep respect and love for the psychoanalytic tradition and a willingness to explore fresh ideas and innovative clinical practices. Their assumption of editorship represents the continual need for change and new contributions. My assumption of the duties of Associate Editor, one year from now, in no way represents a diminution of my involvement with PD, but rather a different role, with less week-to-week administrative responsibility and more involvement in special projects. We all look forward to the continued collaborative process among the editors, and among the editors and authors and readers, that has made Psychoanalytic Dialogues such a rewarding experience for us over the years.

[[RunningHead]]

The Search for Psychological Models

Commentary on Papers by Stephen Seligman and by Robin C. Silverman and Alicia F. Lieberman

E. Demos EDD

E. Demos

E. Demos   Developmental and clinical psychologist whose research has focused on affective development in infancy. She is an assistant clinical professor at the Harvard Medical School, is on the medical staff of the Austen Riggs Center, Stockbridge, MA, and is affiliated with the Cambridge Hospital, Cambridge, MA.

Seligman's and Silverman and Lieberman's Papers Each in its Own way addresses the question of how trauma is passed on from one generation to the next. On the basis of their observations of and therapeutic work with infants, young children, and their parents, these authors argue (a) that the transmission of trauma can be observed in the patterns of transactions that actually occur between parents and their children and (b) that these real experiences then shape and influence the children's intrapsychic phantasies and representations of relationships. In pointing to the actual patterns of early interpersonal experience as an important and legitimate source of data that can inform our understanding of how psychic processes become organized and develop, they join a growing number of researchers and clinicians, including this author, Stern, Beebe, Lachmann, Lyons-Ruth, Fonagy, and other recent attachment writers. They have extended our work by applying this stance to trauma and to the transmission of trauma from one generation to the next.

In essence, these authors are saying that trauma is passed on by traumatized parents traumatizing their own children in spite of conscious efforts on the part of the parents, as in the case of Laura, to protect their children. The authors provide compelling clinical examples to illustrate how trauma gets enacted in real behaviors by the parent and the observable effects these behaviors have on the child.

219

Causality, Adaptation, and Meaning

A Perspective from Attachment Theory and Research

Mauricio Cortina MD

Mauricio Cortina

5 Sunnyside Road
Silver Spring, MD 20910
E-mail: mcortina@aol.com

Mauricio Cortina   Faculty Member at the Washington School of Psychiatry and the Institute of Contemporary Psychotherapy, Associate Professor in the Department of Psychiatry, George Washington University, and a member of Seminario de Socio-Psicoanalisis A. C., Mexico City.

It seems to me fairly clear that the description in terms of meaning and in terms of causal relationships are not alternative and competing, but complementary modes of description [Rubinstein, 1980, p. 508].

Thus the distinction between the natural sciences and historical sciences is not that they use a different method of obtaining knowledge, but that the problems that they strive to understand and the criteria they adopt are quite different. One is concerned with formulating laws in terms of probabilities, the other to understand singular specific events in as much detail as possible [Bowlby, 1988, p. 75].

Current emphasis on the interpretative and interactive nature of the psychoanalytic process has come to question the nature of what we know and what can be known by the psychoanalytic method. There is a wide diversity of opinion regarding these matters. One position, represented by the philosopher Adolph Grünbaum, believes that the psychoanalytic method lacks the epistemic resources to validate its clinical theories. Another view, from analysts who share a hermeneutic

I thank Neil Altman, Marco Bacciagaluppi, Barbara Lenkerd, Michael Maccoby, Jon Frederikson, Alan Sroufe, Mary Main, Morris Eagle, and Lester Luborsky for their helpful comments. Earlier versions of this paper were presented at the fall 1997 scientific meeting of the Institute of Contemporary Psychotherapy, Bethesda, MD, and at the 1998 annual meeting of the American Academy of Psychoanalysis, Toronto, Canada.

557

and constructivist perspective, believes that the main task of psychoanalysis is to expand meaning and purpose. Because meaning is subjective and open to multiple interpretations, the ideal of scientific objectivity and the search for causality are seen as irrelevant to the task of the psychoanalyst. I use prospective and retrospective research informed by attachment theory to address these questions. From a longitudinal perspective, it is possible to discover robust causal connections between the past and the present if the level of analysis is the person's total functioning within specific developmental contexts rather than simple linear continuities in behavior. Retrospective research using the Adult Attachment Interview (AAI) shows that the meaning adults extract from their relationship with their parents also has lawlike qualities. Individuals who are classified secure in the AAI provide more objective evaluations of their relationship with their parents. Their ability to reflect on their experience allows a certain degree of decentering from it, which produces more coherent narratives. In contrast, individuals who are insecure in the AAI produce less coherent, more confused, and contradictory narratives by becoming too preoccupied with their experience or too detached from it. In either case, they show less capacity for reflection. In short, this body or research supports the view that understanding meaning and understanding causal connections are conjunctive rather that disjunctive approaches to knowledge.

Dr. Reis's paper seems to us to embody two aims. One is to explicate the philosophy of Merleau-Ponty and to demonstrate its relevance for relational psychoanalysis, especially for the work of Ogden. This goal the paper achieves admirably. The second aim involves an effort at critical and comparative scholarship, an attempt to show that only Ogden has produced a “truly” post-Cartesian psychoanalytic theory of intersubjectivity, whereas other relational thinkers, such as Benjamin and ourselves, have fallen short. In this goal the paper fails, primarily because Reis has not given the other relational theorists the careful reading he has given to Ogden, his postclassical hero.

Had Reis studied the evolution of our work as carefully as he did Ogden's, for example, he would know that he is incorrect in characterizing our theory of intersubjectivity as having “arise[n] out of self psychology.

This paper addresses the gender of the analyst as an important factor in the shaping of the analytic experience. Specifically, it focuses on the notion that female analysts bring something different from what male analysts bring to the analytic dyad and that this influences and shapes the analysts' translation of the patients' experience—determining treatment course and therapeutic enactments. It begins with the idea that, for some men, working with a female analyst may case the processing and metabolization of their aggression—allowing for a (re) integration of it with their sexuality and desire. The clinical material of a male analysand who sought treatment from a woman analyst is used to explore this idea within a post-Lacanian framework incorporating Kristeva's work on the maternal chora, the imaginary father or third, and the journey that the infant must make from the real to the symbolic, from biology to desire.

THE CULTURE OF PSYCHOANALYSIS HAS UNDERGONE A RADICAL CHANGE during the past two decades. The bursting forth of object-relational, interpersonal, intersubjective, self-psychological, and other views has invigorated psychoanalysis with an excitement lacking in the recent past. For those of us who grew up as rebels during a period of orthodoxy, the current era of theoretical pluralism has been a liberation and a blessing. However, all liberations carry with them new sets of problems, and Lewis Aron's paper is a wise discourse on some of the issues that now confront us. Aron, continuing his important work on psychoanalytic technique, here addresses some of these knotty problems of our time and also reveals that there may be significant deficiencies in our current capacity to answer the questions that have been raised. His paper is wise, witty, a joy to read, and difficult to discuss because I am in general agreement with so much of what he says. To mention a few of the issues he raises:

He asks, “What is left of the foundations upon which we may build a theory of psychoanalytic technique?” And states, “The very foundations of our discipline have come into question.” Even more strongly, “We disagree with one another to the core because psychoanalysis is so thoroughly value laden. The Creation of A Bridge Between Melanie Klein'S (1946, 1955) once arcane, recondite, and phantasmal conception of projective identification and current empirical infant development research constitutes a landmark in the integration of psychoanalysis with empirical research. The first bridge was forged by Wilfred Bion (1959, 1962) in his formulation of maternal reverie, the container, and the contained, in which he decisively lifted Klein's idea from its strictly intrapsychic moorings as a strictly unconscious infantile phantasy to the status of a necessary (for the infant) mode of communication of affects. The next step had to come from infant development research. Dr. Seligman's paper goes a long way in its laudable attempt to close the gap between the current tenets of postmodern intersubjective psychoanalytic concepts and those of Klein and her followers by continuing the trend started by Bion.

Drs. Silverman and Lieberman have written a poignantly graphic psychoanalytic “documentary” for the now burgeoning literature on trauma and abuse, a theme that challenges the foundations of the still highly intrapsychic orientation of psychoanalysis.

Seligman suggests that two factors aid this bridge building: “(a) the image of the infant-parent dyad as a two-person, mutual-influence, mutual-regulation system and (b) new understandings of nonverbal patternings … of the internal structures of meaning that are organized in preverbal, affective, chorographic, and kinesthetic dimensions of self-with-other experience.” He cites Stern's (1985) and Beebe and Lachmann's (1988) descriptions of affect attunements, moments of disruption and repair, and “the moment-to-moment rhythms and tonalities that give both parent-infant and clinical interactions their distinctive feel.” He wants psychoanalysis to add a concentration on form to its more usual preoccupation with content, and he thinks that the Kleinian emphasis on unconscious phantasy—if only it can be loosed from its earlier bodily imagery, its psychic primitivity, its negativity, and, in particular, its tendency to ascribe too much responsibility to the patient—offers a rich set of descriptors, full of evocative intensity, of the subtle and complex “formattings” of dyadic influence.

FREUD METAPHORIZED ANALYTIC TECHNIQUE AS AN INSTRUMENT—THE surgeon's scalpel. I regard the analytic instrument more as a cello. In his thoughtful article, Aron asks how—in this postmodern age of theoretical pluralism and “irreducible subjectivity”—do we play our instrument and how do we teach others a sound technique? Let us consider these questions as a matter of musicality.

A case in point. Because our eight-year-old granddaughter, Lindsey, was studying the cello, and because Yo-Yo Ma was her hero, my wife and I wrote a letter to Mr. Ma. We asked him whether, as a neighbor and fellow Harvard alumnus, he would consider our request to offer Lindsey a cello lesson when she came east from Vancouver for the Christmas holidays—at whatever fee he charged. Mr. Ma's secretary phoned us to say he would happily receive Lindsey and her cello but that he would accept no fee because, “when he was a young learner, others were similarly kind to him.” Ma loves music, he loves children, and he loves to teach.

So, when Lindsey arrived in mid-December, she and her father walked to Ma's house at the prearranged hour. Ma greeted Lindsey with his broad smile and asked what she was studying. Her father produced her music, a simple folk tune, and Ma ran upstairs for his cello. He spent the hour joining Lindsey in playing her music. Lindsey came home beaming. She described with particular delight one pointer that Ma had offered her: he said that he knew a way that the bow could play both the first and last cello strings at the same time, a feat considered impossible because of the curvature of the bridge.

A gendered person lives in and creates a gendered body. Through the sexual life of the couple, two gendered bodies come together. In this paper, I draw on case material to illustrate how a multitude of sensations associated with what it feels like to be a man or a woman is aroused, affirmed, and created as one dimension of sexual experience. I suggest that these gender sensations may be organized through the three psychological positions—autistic-contiguous, paranoid-schizoid, and depressive. The movement among all three positions gives different shapes to sensory-based gender experience—each position making its own unique contributions to the qualities of what it feels like to be a man or a woman. In one case, I consider the possibility that gender transformation occurred through an unconscious intersubjective erotic third1—the body of the couple—created between the patient and her husband.
